videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here on Penang Island in Malaysia. It's known for its tasty food, beautiful nature, and cool street art. So in this video we're going to show you the best things to do here. Hope you enjoy it! Penang is a state in the west of Malaysia, consisting of a tropical island and a peninsula connected by two bridges. The island has become a popular tourist destination thanks to its colonial architecture, natural and cultural attractions, and some of the best street food in Asia. George Town is Penang's capital and it's a great place to explore the island from. In this video, we'll highlight 10 of the best things to do in Penang, taking you to all different corners of the island. Now let's dive in! We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Penang is well-known for its amazing street food scene. The best way to experience this firsthand is to visit one of the hawker centers, which are a collection of food stalls. You can find them all around Penang and dishes you should try include Lakse, Cendol, and Nasi Kandar. In the heart of George Town you can also find the Chulia Street Night Market, where you can sample some delicious Chinese-Malaysian food. But if you want to learn more about the Malay food and flavors, you can also join a street food tour. You can find a tour link in the description below this video. George Town is home to a stunning mix of European and Asian architecture. It's no wonder that this city received UNESCO World Heritage Site status in 2008. When walking around this historical area, you'll find lots of beautiful buildings and wall paintings, which represent images from Penang's past and daily life. You can find the most popular murals on Google Maps, but it's fun to just wander around the town and stumble upon them as well. Make sure to stop by the historic Chinese Clan Temple and Museum, with detailed architecture, murals, and stone carvings. One of the most popular things to do on Penang Island is to take the 2 kilometer or 1.2 mile funicular up to Penang Hill. Once at the top, you have a panoramic view of the city and mainland in the distance. But there's a lot more to enjoy than just the views. You can also find a Hindu temple, a mosque, a variety of nature trails, an art gallery, and even cafés and food courts. It's easy to spend a few hours here, but be aware that the queues to take the funicular can be very long. It's also possible to hike steep stairs to the top, but be prepared for a sweaty hike if you choose that option. Our favorite attraction on Penang Hill deserves its own mention. The Habitat is a Rainforest Discovery Center that takes you to the heart of the jungle. It's a treasure of biodiversity, boasting an abundance of wildlife. You can explore the forest by walking a 1.6 kilometer or 1 mile long nature trail, including informational signs, a canopy walk, and a viewing platform. We were lucky to spot dusky leaf monkeys in the trees, which was a highlight for us. So if you're walking in the rainforest out here, you'll hear a lot of loud sounds. They're actually mostly coming from bugs that are very small. The Clan Jetties are one of the most important historical areas in Penang. Located at the waterfront, this traditional village consists of residential houses built on stilts right over the water. This unique type of settlement was created by Chinese immigrants in the late 19th century. And there are now 6 jetties that you can visit, and Chew Jetty is the most popular one, with many souvenir shops and places to eat. While the jetties are technically private property where families live, most of them welcome visitors. Another interesting and unique neighborhood in George Town is Little India. The temple Sri Mahamariamman marks the start of the area and behind it, you'll find colorful streets with goldsmiths and stores selling sarees and trinkets. But what we love the most was the abundance of restaurants, serving tasty and authentic Indian food. The most impressive temple on Penang Island is undoubtedly Kek Lok Si. This is the largest Buddhist temple in Malaysia, covering an area of over 12 hectares or 29 acres. The temple is made up of colorful prayer halls, beautiful gardens, ponds with fish and turtles, an impressive seven-tiered pagoda, and a large bronze statue of the Buddhist Goddess of Compassion. It's free to enter, but be aware that it's located on a hill so you'll have to walk some steep steps to get around the temple. There's also an inclined elevator that carries visitors to the top where the tall statue sits. From here, you have some great views of the city. Located in the northwest corner of the island, Penang National Park is the smallest National Park in Malaysia. There are three walking trails that cut through the jungle here. We decided to take the right fork to head to Monkey Beach, which is the most popular and easiest of the three trails since it's relatively flat. We put a link to our trail route in the description below the video if you want to follow it on your phone when visiting the park. However, trail condition, humidity, and heat made this a challenging workout. It's also possible to get to Monkey Beach by boat and we actually decided to take the boat back to the entrance at the end of the day. One of the unfortunate things here is the amount of plastic that's just washed up on the shore as you can see behind me. So think twice when you carry a bottle of water and don't just throw it away in nature. If you want to escape the heat of the city, you don't have to go too far. Batu Ferringhi Beach is only a half hour drive from George Town and has powdery soft sands and turquoise waters to enjoy. The beach is lined with a mix of beautiful accommodations, bars, entertainment venues, and plenty of dining options. It's also a great place to watch sunset and at night you can enjoy the big night market here to try more delicious street food. Finally, our favorite place to visit on Penang Island was Balik Pulau in the west. This hidden gem is covered by beautiful greenery, paddy fields, and rolling hills. It's less populated and less developed than the rest of the island, making it the perfect getaway from city life. We explored the area by joining a half-day guided cycling tour. Our guide Eddie led us through rural areas and villages on lesser-explored paths. While enjoying the scenic countryside, we also learned about the history and culture of Penang and Malaysia. We also visited a traditional fishing village and dairy farm. You can find more information about the tour via the link in the description below the video. At the end of the ride, we got to ask our guide why it's important to visit local areas when traveling in Malaysia. There are a few things to do in Penang that we didn't mention and we've included a list of those on the screen here for you. If you're planning your Malaysia trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code below. We also included important links that you'll need for your Penang trip in the description, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

videos.transcript: Hello travelers, welcome back to our channel. Known as the pearl of the Southern Seas, Penang sits at the crossroads between the East and the West. It is renowned for its lush vegetation, mouthwatering food and diverse architecture, but there are much more things to love Penang for. If you succumb to the island’s allure on your trip to Penang in 2023, first check out our "top ten things to know before going to Penang. Penang Travel Requirements Following in the wake of Thailand, Malaysia has opened up to the world. Even though in 2022 the tourist numbers went nowhere near the pre-pandemic level, 2023 promises to be different. There are no travel restrictions to enter Malaysia. You don’t have to either be vaccinated or declare your vaccine status, nor do you have to wear a mask while in public. Note that whereas most Westerners can spend up to 3 months in Malaysia without a visa, other nations enjoy only a month-long visa waiver, while some need to apply for a Malaysian visa in advance. Contact your closest Malaysian embassy if you have any doubts. As my personal experience goes, Penang has been among my favourite destinations for border runs from Thailand. Even though many expatriates in Southern Thailand choose to go on a 1-to-2 day visa run, Penang deserves more, while also featuring a Thai consulate of its own. How to get to Penang? Transportation is not an issue when it comes to getting to Penang! Entering Penang from both mainland Malaysia and abroad is straightforward. You can fly to Penang International Airport, which is served by low-cost carriers like Air Asia and is known for its high connectivity and cutting-edge infrastructure. Flights are available not only from Southeast Asia's transport hubs like Bangkok and Kuala Lumpur but also from far-off destinations like the Gulf. Alternatively, you can drive to Penang. Take a taxi from Haad Yai, Thailand, and drive to Penang through the Thai-Malay border. The ride from Haad Yai to Penang lasts just four hours and costs approximately $20. If neither option suits you, you can take a train to Penang. To travel to Penang from abroad by train, take a train to Penang from Thailand. You can travel from Bangkok, the capital of Thailand, to Penang by train via Padang Besar Crossing. Jump off the train and hop over the border there to board a Komuter Train on the Malay side of the border. Or, if you are in the mood for a romantic trip to Penang, you can sail to Penang from the Langkawi Islands: a pristine archipelago known for its primeval forest, idyllic stretches of sand, boutique accommodation, and customs-free alcohol. When is it best time to visit Penang? While it is suitable to visit Penang all year round, my personal favorite is February. In February, Penangites celebrate Chinese New Year and Taipussan, a traditional Hindu festival. You can join the crowd in one of the Chinese New Year festivities not only at Kek Lok Si Temple but also at Ban Ka Lan Snake Temple; participate in Pai Thee Kong Celebration (Jade Emperor's Birthday); or witness Chap Goh Meh, one of the unique Penang-only Chinese New Year rituals. Yet another reason to make a trip to Penang in February is the Indian Festival of Thaipusam. The streets are filled with colorful processions, music, and dance performances. People carry kavadis decorated with flowers and pierce their bodies as a form of devotion. If you can’t come here in February, don’t regret not being able to make it. There are other occasions that are worth taking part in Penang: Deepavali, or festival of light. The festival is celebrated on 12 November in 2023, and is one of the best carnivals to attend in Penang. Little India will be lit up with colorful lights, and people come from all over to enjoy the festive atmosphere. It's an awesome way to experience the Indian culture and have a great time in Penang! 4 . How long to stay in Penang? Some may argue that a weekend is enough to explore the cultural heritage of Georgetown, but it is not enough to take in all there is on the island. Head to Penang for five days to a week, to travel not only Georgetown, the island’s capital, but also to see Penang nature marvels, which include Penang Hill, and other Penang highlights such as Batu Ferringhi Beach, to add to myriad historical landmarks that Penang island is renowned for. 5. Where to stay in Penang? The simple answer is Georgetown! Even though there are really nice spots to lodge throughout Penang, it is best to check into one of the hotels in Georgetown due to their close proximity to the island’s principal historical sites. What is more, the island’s main appeal lies in its cuisine, and it is here in downtown that you can tuck into Penang’s best grub. But if you prefer peaceful beach gateaways to bustling nightlife, you can consider Batu Ferringhi. Batu Ferringhi is a stunning beach with picturesque scenery. There are plenty of accommodation options here, from luxurious resorts to cozy bungalows. You can check our of our videos here for more about the “Best Hotels in Penang”. 6. What to eat in Penang Penang is a food lover's paradise, with a melting pot of cultures that has resulted in a rich and diverse culinary scene. Here are just a few of the must-try dishes when visiting this foodie haven: Char Kway Teow: This popular noodle dish is made with flat rice noodles, stir-fried with prawns, cockles, bean sprouts, eggs, and Chinese sausage. This is a must-try dish in Penang Assam Laksa: Another famous and special Penang dish. It's a sour and tangy fish-based soup that's packed with flavors from tamarind, lemongrass, and galangal. The soup is served with rice noodles, shredded fish, and a variety of vegetables. Hokkien Mee: Hokkien Mee is a noodle dish that's made with thick yellow noodles and thin rice vermicelli. It's served in a rich broth made with prawns, pork, and soy sauce, and garnished with crispy fried shallots, chili paste, and lime. Nasi Kandar: This is a popular dish that originated from the northern state of Malaysia. It's a simple dish of steamed rice served with various curries, meat, and vegetables. The dish is known for its rich, spicy, and aromatic flavors. Our favourite restaurant to have Nasi Khandar is Hameediyah Restaurant. Rojak: Rojak is a popular fruit salad that's made with a mix of tropical fruits like pineapple, mango, and papaya, tossed in a sweet and sour sauce made of shrimp paste, sugar, and lime juice. It's topped with peanuts and crispy croutons for a crunchy texture. We have another video showcasing some of the best dining spots in Penang. You can check it out here or in description section. 7. Top things to do in Penang As Penang is a melting pot of cultures and history, it has a variety of experiences for visitors. You can start by exploring Georgetown, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, where you can discover the history, art, and culture of the city while indulging in local street food. Then take a funicular train ride up to Penang Hill for stunning views of the island and a peaceful walk through the lush green forests. Near Penang Hill is Kek Lok Si Temple, one of the largest Buddhist temples in Southeast Asia, with impressive architecture, beautiful gardens, and statues. Finally for a beach experience, head to Batu Ferringhi. We have another video featuring in-depth coverage of the top things to do in Penang. Check it out in here. I also put the links in the description. 8. How to move around in Penang The main city of George Town is completely walkable so long as you wear light clothes and don't forget your sunblock. Note that you can get almost anywhere in Penang by public transport. But be more mindful of the traffic. For you to avoid congestion, make sure it is in the morning that you go on your trip of Penang’s nature or culture attractions. Keep in mind that Grab drivers may not be available at night, so prepare to pay extra for your nocturnal ride to the airport or elsewhere. If you prefer to have more mobility, renting a bike or car is a good option. Just remember that driving is on the left side of the road. 9. How much does it cost to travel to Penang Thanks to affordable airfare, traveling to Penang has never been easier. You can fly from KL, Bangkok, and even Surat Thani, Thailand, for as little as $20-50. However, other expenses will determine how much you spend on your trip. Accommodation is the most significant expense, with options ranging from budget to luxurious. A decent 3-star hotel costs around $30 per night, while 4-star and 5-star hotels average around $60 and $100 per night, respectively. Food in Penang is very affordable. A plate of Nasi Kandar with beef at Hameediyah costs only 14 ringgit, around $3. Eating out for dinner can range from 2 ringgit for glass noodles to 7 ringgit for Chinese fried noodles with seafood, including octopus! 10. Some additional tips Here are some additional tips to travel to Penang. Don't miss the chance to buy real Chinese tea in Penang, which is known to be one of the best places to do so. Check out Huang Chen Hao's tea salon on Campbell Street for unique Chinese green Pu Er tea. While you're there, also take advantage of the prime Chinese medicine healthcare available in the area. Visit Cheng Wah Medical Hall, located across from a Chinese tea shop, to purchase traditional Chinese medicine. Or you can try acupuncture at the Traditional Chinese Medicine Clinics. There are quite a few good ones. An hour of acupuncture at a prime medical facility in Penang will cost you just $10 per session, simply the world’s best-value! Note that prices for expat medical care here are significantly lower than in Thailand, making Penang a burgeoning medical tourism destination! Thanks for watching! If you enjoyed this Penang travel guide and want to see more content like this, please give this video a thumbs up and hit the subscribe button for more travel guides and adventures. Happy travel and see you in the next video!

All right, now let's talk about the best places to visit while you're in Malaysia. So right now I am here in Malacca. This is actually a very nice old town. But also while you're here in Malaysia everyone goes to the capital Kuala Lumpur. That's probably right up there with Malacca as my two favorites that I've been. I've also been to JB which is Johor Bahru right there with Singapore. Not much going on there to be honest with you but it's up and coming. It has a lot of potential if you want to check it out. And then if you go up into the mountains from there you have the Cameron Highlands and then from Kuala Lumpur you have also the Genting Highlands. So lots of Highlands out there. And then there's Penang, which they say has the best food in all of Malaysia. Also, it's got the old historical area known as Georgetown, where they also have a decent nightlife around Chulia Street. Okay, let's talk about transportation. So, as this boat here goes by, this is a boat that you have here on the river in Malacca. But, they do have trains. Especially on the Malaysian Peninsula. Airports in Johor, airports in Penang, Langkawi, airports in Kuala Lumpur, that's the big one. Airports in KK, air which is Kota Kinabalu. Airports in Sarawak. So, that's all Borneo. But, then you have Grab. Grab is like the main way you'd want to get around if you're in KL, you're going to use the MRT light rail. They also have a subway. So, that's how I get around as well as taxis. But, here in Malacca, I'm going to be using Grab and most places use Grab as well. But, check out this boat here. Now, we'll actually talk about the currency. So, it's a Malaysian ringgit. One US dollar equals 4.45 Malaysian ringgit at the time of making this video. You can easily get that money from a currency exchange at the airport or at ATMs. The only ATM machine that I've had problems with was Maybank. Not saying it's uh it could just be my luck or timing. But, that was the only one that I ever had a problem with. Uh the problem with the Euronet or Euro Bank ATM is they charge a lot of fees. So, uh unfortunately, you're going to be getting a lot of fees when going to the ATMs and currency conversion's not ideal, but you got to have money. Also, they do take mostly Visa, not very many American Express. So, take that into consideration with what's expected. All right, now let's talk about safety here in Malaysia. So, I've gone all around the Malaysian Peninsula. I've gone to so many different places. I would say, generally speaking, it's a very safe country. It's a totally different vibe than what you get in Thailand, which is also a safe country for the most part. But, I would say it's very safe I for tourists. Even with locals, I don't think the crime is very high. The things that you might want to consider as far as safety, the heat. That's something that I've had to deal with, but uh the roads are in good condition, they're modern. It seems like Malaysia just keeps getting better and better with time. Uh but if you were to look at criminal statistics or crime data, it's very safe. I mean, look at it. You can look online. Uh so, you know, I wasn't sure what to expect. I was you know, I came over here, I was kind of concerned, but as time's gone by, I've gotten more and more uh happy with it. And uh I guess some some neighborhoods maybe in like Kuala Lumpur might be a little bit iffy. Or maybe in JB in Johor Bahru, but for the most part, it's a very safe country. So, that's what I would like to say. Don't have too much to worry about. Take general safety precautions. All right, now let's talk about Malaysian cuisine. It's a fusion between local Malay food, Chinese, Indian. You can see they've got nasi goreng, nasi lemak, uh which is typically like a rice dish. Then, you've got mee goreng, which is going to be noodle. Anytime you see something that says mee, m e e. When you go to places like Penang or Kuala Lumpur, you're going to have a lot of Chinese style dishes because Chinese food is very common down here. You also have a variety of different Indian restaurants, and even some kebab and shawarma options. You'll see that a lot in Kuala Lumpur, especially in Bukit Bintang. So, my favorite street, if you really want to indulge, is going to be down on Jalan Alor, right there in Bukit Bintang in Kuala Lumpur. So, head down there and you can walk that whole street and see so many different food options. So, make sure if you love food, you got to go to Jalan Alor. All right, let's talk a little bit of history about Malaysia. So, at one point, there was lower sea levels and Malaysia, the area of Malaysian Peninsula and Borneo were actually connected by a land bridge that also connected up towards Cambodia, Java, and Sumatra. That was when there was lower sea levels. Then, about 10,000 years ago, after the ice age, the sea levels rose and then you have the Gulf of Siam, you have the Gulf of Thailand, and these other areas. At one point in time, it was noted by the Chinese that there was an advanced empire in Indochina around the area of southern Cambodia called Funan, where the Chinese traders said it was a very advanced civilization. Now, along with Chinese traders at the time, there was also signs and evidence that Roman traders were actually in the area trading with southern Cambodia, Funan Empire. It's interesting to point out that that's south of Angkor Wat. So, there was another civilization in Cambodia where Angkor Wat is under King Suryavarman. But, sticking to the idea of Malaysia, down here, they had the empire of Malacca, which was established around the 14th and 15th century. And about around that time, Chinese were coming down in large numbers, immigrants, which are called Peranakan or Straits Chinese. They were settling here to do a lot of the working. They were working around here along with the trade, as well as Arabic traders who came from Saudi Arabia and that area. And they spread Islam here and that's how in the 1500s, Malaysia became mostly Islamic because they converted the emperor or the king, the sultan of Malacca to become Muslim. Before Islam, it was mostly Hindu and Buddhist when the Malacca Empire was in its early years. Now, let's talk about weather in Malaysia. videos.description:
What are the pros and cons of moving to Penang? Let's go over that in this video!

In this video, we are examining the pros and cons of moving to Penang. I will place timestamps to detail the pros and cons mentioned in the video.

videos.transcript: What are the pros and cons of moving to Penang? In this video, we'll talk about that. Hello my friend, my name is Timothy. For over two decades, I've been known for curating information on Penang through my website Penang Travel Tips. Now this YouTube channel serves as an extension of that work in meeting a specific need, which is to assist people in the journey of relocating to Penang and ensuring a seamless transition. Year after year Penang attracts thousands of newcomers both from within Malaysia and beyond. Its thriving job market and diverse opportunities draw individuals from various walks of life including expatriates seeking short term engagements with multi-national corporations and retirees seeking tranquility, whether or not they are part of the MM2H program. Recognizing the lack of a dedicated platform to guide these newcomers, I've launched this channel with the sole purpose of providing invaluable assistance. If you're considering Penang as your new home I extend a warm welcome and my aim is to make you feel right at home from the moment you arrive. Nestled along the northwest coast of Malaysia, Penang often referred to as the Pearl of the Orient is a captivating blend of old-world charm and modern vibrancy. This island state boasts a rich tapestry of cultural heritage evident in its colonial architecture, bustling street markets and mouthwatering culinary delights. Penang's strategic location has made it a melting pot of cultures with influences from Malay, Chinese, Indian and European traditions seamlessly intertwined. From the bustling streets of George Town, recognized by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site, renowned for its historic buildings and colorful street art, to the tranquil beaches of Batu Ferringhi, there's something to enchant every newcomer. Beyond is cultural allure, Penang offers a high quality of life with excellent healthcare facilities world class education options and a well-developed infrastructure. Whether you're seeking adventure in its lush tropical jungles, relaxation on its pristine beaches or immersion in its vibrant cultural scene, Penang welcomes you with open arms, ready to embrace you as part of its diverse and dynamic community. I was born in Penang and I have lived in Penang my entire life and what I offer you through this channel is Penang through the lens of a native Penangite. Before we dive deeper into exploring Penang, I want to be transparent with you: I am a real estate professional with a team of real estate agents. My agents assist individuals in finding their ideal property, whether to buy or rent, helping them enjoy a smooth and seamless relocation to Penang. Having said that, the purpose of this channel is not so much to promote our real estate services, but rather to provide you with information and insights to make accurate and informed decision about moving to Penang. Through this series of videos, I hope to help you navigate the process of relocating to Penang with confidence, and avoid any surprises or unexpected hurdles. So while my team is here to offer our assistance should you require it our main focus is to equip you with knowledge and resources you need to embark on your Penang journey with clarity and peace of mind. Let's embark on this adventure together armed with information and a shared goal of making Penang your new home. Okay, with that out of the way, let's get back to the topic at hand. Penang is one of Asia's top travel destinations. It is also one of the choice locations for people contemplating retirement in Asia. Malaysians from other states also come to Penang for its wide selection of job opportunities. But is Penang for you? Let's go through some of the pros and cons of moving to Penang. First of all, Penang is renowned for food. It is often regarded as the food capital of Malaysia, and Penang food is outstanding and well known throughout the world. We will go deeper into Penang food in a separate video, but in terms of food variety and taste is a "pro". Secondly, and this pertains to foreigners, Penang boasts a vibrant expat community, making it easy for you to find like-minded individuals and build a support network. Additionally, foreigners find the cost of living in Penang to be relatively affordable compared to other major cities in the region. From delicious street food to world class health facilities Penang offers a high quality of life at a fraction of the cost. So that's another "pro" for moving to Penang. For those who wanting to escape harsh winters, here's another pro: Penang's tropical climate means you can enjoy sunshine and warm weather year-round. That's perfect for outdoor enthusiast and beach lovers. And let's not forget about the rich cultural tapestry that Penang is known for, from colorful festivals to historical landmarks, there's always something new to explore here. Now having sold you on the pros of moving to Penang, let's not disregard the cons. A major downside of Penang is the traffic congestion especially during peak hours. Navigating Penang streets can be a bit overwhelming so it's important to plan your commute accordingly. In separate videos, we will go into the details of where to stay if you're coming to Penang to work as a local from another state or as an expat from another country or you are here as a retiree. The second con is while English is widely spoken and understood there may still be language barriers, and the average level of English is not on par with places like, say, Singapore. Although foreigners generally find the cost of living in Penang to be affordable, that's not immediately the case with people from other states who often find housing in Penang to be more expensive than anywhere in Malaysia aside from Kuala Lumpur and Johor Bahru. So that's a con for those from other states. Another con is land scarcity in Penang, necessitating the construction of high-rise housing with 50-storey apartments becoming increasingly common. If you're accustomed to staying in landed property, this is something you may have to get used to if you're moving to Penang. Moving to Penang offers a multitude of benefits from its diverse culture to its affordable cost of living. However, it's essential to weigh the pros and cons carefully to ensure that Penang is the right fit for you. Remember every individual's experience may vary so be sure to do your research and consider your own priorities before making the move. In future videos, we will dive deeper into specific aspects of moving to and living in Penang so I can help you integrate yourself to our local society. Penang people are generally warm and friendly and we are eager to help you make Penang your new home. That's all for today's video. If you find it helpfulbe sure to give it a thumbs up and subscribe to Penang My New Home for more tips and insights on living in Penang. Until next time thanks for watching!
